items.q: How do you say 'I play the piano' vs 'I play football'?
items.correct: Je joue DU piano (instrument) vs je joue AU football (sport).
items.misconception: Same preposition for both - 'je joue piano', 'je joue football'.
items.random_wrong: Je joue le piano, je joue le football.
items.why: Students drop or confuse prepositions. The rule: jouer DE + instrument (de, du, de la); jouer À + sport/game (à, au, aux). Easy way to lose marks in speaking exams about hobbies.
A classic faux ami. Actuellement looks like 'actually' but means CURRENTLY. To say 'actually' use en fait or en réalité. These false friends regularly cost marks.
Students use 'ends in -e = feminine' as a rule. Many exceptions exist: le problème, le système, le programme, le poème - all masculine despite the -e ending. Gender must often be memorised.

items.q: Who directed À bout de souffle (1960)?
items.why: It's Godard. “Truffaut” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: passé simple. “passé composé” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Les Quatre Cents Coups released?
items.why: Exact answer: 1959. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
